data.title: Bank7 Starts Off 2026 With Impressive Q1
data.description:
Earnings season for the banking industry officially kicks off this week with several large national and smaller regional banks reporting their financial results from Q1 2026. One of the companies that falls into that second category, with a market cap of just $408.6 million, is Bank7 Corporation (BSVN). If its recent press release is any indication of a wider trend, it could be a good month to be invested in the regional banking industry.

BSVN earned $1.25 per share in the first three months of this year, topping expectations by $0.23 per share, or 22.5%. That EPS total, as well as the company’s net income of $12.0 million, was a record for Bank7. As part of the press release that included a full report of the bank’s recent operations, Thomas Travis, President and CEO of Bank7, stated, “We are excited about 2026, as our properly matched balance sheet has us well positioned to continue to take advantage of our dynamic geographic region.”

For Bank7, this strong start to the year was a continuation of an impressive 2025. Many of the company’s operational metrics are well above-average. Any concerns that the economic backdrop from the start of this year, especially in the month of March, may have dragged down all members of the regional banking industry appear unwarranted. Even though BSVN has gained over 4% today on the news of its recent quarterly results, I still believe the stock is worth a Buy.

Company Overview

Based in Oklahoma City, Bank7 Corporation is the holding company for Bank7, which provides banking and other financial services to consumer and commercial clients. The company operates eight branches in its home state, two in Kansas, and two in Texas. In spite of its small size, Bank7 specializes in serving businesses in a few niche industries like energy, hospitality, and agriculture. The company claims to have a competitive advantage of fewer but “higher-quality” bankers that can accomplish more with a smaller branch footprint.

At the end of Q1 2026, BSVN reported $1.95 billion in total assets that included a loan portfolio of $1.59 billion. Both of these numbers were slight declines from Q4 2025 of less than 1%. However, when compared to Q1 2025, total assets at the bank have increased 8.9%. Total loans have risen 11.9%.

A breakdown of the loan mix for the bank is shown in the pie chart below. The largest share of loans is connected to commercial and industrial loans at $407.4 million, or 25.5%. The commitment to those niche industries mentioned earlier can be seen here. Commercial real estate allocations are split between investor CRE at 9.9%, owner-occupied CRE at 8.4%, and hospitality properties at 19.0%. Hospitality CRE would include loans to hotels, resorts, and other businesses in the hospitality industry. Bank7 also has exposure to small segments of energy and agricultural loans.

Bank7 currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.27 per share, which gives its stock a forward yield of 2.52%. The company currently has a streak of six straight years of annual payout increases. Its last dividend raise came in August of last year when it boosted its distribution by $0.03 per share. Over the last five years, the compound average growth rate of its annual dividend is just over 20%. The bank’s payout ratio of 21.6% is well below the average for other dividend-paying banks, showing that further increases in the future are likely.

Analysis of Bank7 Corporation

The most recent quarterly results for Bank7 included a number of strengths for the company that go beyond just its bottom line. However, it is important to note that the $1.25 EPS was 11.6% better than Q4 2025 and 15.7% higher than Q1 2025. The most recent earnings were produced from core operations without the benefit of any share repurchases.

Most all of the performance ratios reported by the bank are well ahead of peer banks with similar assets. Bank7’s yield on earning assets is 7.35%, supported by a loan yield of 8.03%, which is one of the highest I have seen in all of the regional banks I have written about on Seeking Alpha. The last report from the FDIC said that the average bank with assets between $1 billion and $10 billion had a loan yield of just 6.56%. Bank7's high loan yield is a big factor behind its net interest margin of 5.27%. This NIM is about 136 basis points above the FDIC-stated average.

Other strengths for the company include remarkable expense controls that have kept its efficiency ratio at or below 40% for five consecutive years. In Q1 2026, that number was just 39.64%. Return on assets in Q1 2026 was 2.56%. That number was 30 basis points better than Q4 2025 and almost twice the most recent national average.

Even with its somewhat unique and high-yielding loan portfolio, Bank7 has been able to maintain solid asset quality ratios for the last couple of years. In Q1 2026, its ratio for nonperforming assets was 0.48%, slightly higher than Q4 2025, but still well within a normal range. Nonperforming loans also increased from the previous quarter to 0.58%, but the bank was able to avoid having to report any net charge-offs in the last two quarters of results. The company actually was able to report a small recovery of about $50,000 in Q1 2026.

One other positive to report for the bank is its strong shareholder alignment with insider ownership. Insiders own approximately 55% of the company’s outstanding stock. Institutions own an additional 34%. This leaves only a little more than 10% for public ownership outside of those two groups.

Valuation Consideration

BSVN trades at a trailing PE of about 9.5, below the median for its sector. The company is expected to earn $4.36 in FY 2026, which would actually be a slight decrease from the $4.45 reported for FY 2025. However, Q1 2026 marked the 13th consecutive quarter that the company has beaten its analysts' expectations, and given the metrics discussed earlier, I see no reason to believe that trend will change in the near future.

Last year, Bank7 was able to grow its tangible book value per share to $25.26. That was a 16.4% improvement from the end of 2024 and in line with the company's performance over the last seven years. Since 2018, the compound average growth rate for BSVN's tangible book value is 16.8%.

At its current price of about $44 per share, Bank7 trades at a price-to-TBV multiple of 1.74. This is higher than the national average for all banks, which was about 1.45 at the end of FY 2025. However, the bank's profitability and strong operational metrics would seem to merit this higher valuation.

Risks to Consider

While the uniqueness of Bank7’s loan portfolio is certainly considered a strength because of the high yield it produces for the company, it could also be a weakness if the industries in which it is concentrated begin to struggle. The hospitality industry could come under pressure if consumer spending declines later this year or labor costs were to increase significantly. The energy sector, especially for oil and other natural resources, is undergoing a great deal of turmoil currently because of the conflict in the Middle East. Higher oil prices could be a boon for many companies that borrow money from Bank7, but this price spike may be relatively short-lived. The agricultural sector in many parts of the US is facing pressure from higher costs and lower market prices for their crops. In short, the exposure that Bank7 has to these different sectors bears watching in the future quarters of results.

Like a few other small regional banks that I have covered in the last year, BSVN is relatively thinly traded. There were 12 days in March when fewer than 10,000 shares of the stock traded hands. Even on April 14, when Q1 2026 earnings were released, Seeking Alpha showed a trading volume of less than 24,000 shares. This lack of activity can often cause wider spreads between the bid and the ask in the open market. Limit orders are suggested when making a purchase in Bank7 Corporation. If an investor needs to liquidate a position in a short period of time, a market sale could push the price down significantly lower than the previous transaction, depending on the size of the transaction itself.

The Bottom Line

There really is little reason for pessimism about Bank7’s prospects for the next quarter and the second half of the year. Interest rate concerns and economic headwinds may impact all banks at different times in 2026, but BSVN’s strong recent performance moves it to the front of its industry in my book. For investors who are considering additional exposure to small-cap banks, Bank7 is worth a longer look.

data.title: BlackRock Is A Buy After Q1 Despite Private Credit Exposure
data.description:
Earlier this year, I reiterated my buy rating on BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) for the second straight time, citing strong net inflows, an aggressive push into alternatives, and a valuation that was still attractive. Since then, a private credit crisis of confidence has unfolded, and a war in the Middle East has arisen. Therefore, while the stock's ~7% declines since the publication of my previous article are undesirable for the bulls, I would say that BlackRock has held up alright, all things considered. The asset management giant reported their 2026 Q1 results just this morning (April 14th), and so today, I will be providing a much-needed update for readers.

Below, it is shown that BlackRock's Q1 was another strong quarter for the firm. Net flows were overall solid, and operating margin expansion showed that the business is getting well run. BlackRock has continued its move into alternatives, and while private credit is definitely a risk worth monitoring, in the grand scheme of things their exposure is nowhere near terrifying. With the valuation below where it was in my previous article, I believe a reiteration of my buy rating is justified.

Net Flows Show Resilience

As always, a deep dive into their net flow stats should give us a good understanding of where their business is right now and the risk appetite of investors. For the firm as a whole, they saw net inflows of $130 billion. That is more than we saw in the year-ago quarter, and you can see above that the LTM organic asset growth rate has remained strong at 6%. An even more encouraging sign is the fact that LTM organic base fee growth accelerated from the previous quarter. In fact, growth has been on a steady uptrend to show that BlackRock is monetizing their huge AUM in an increasingly effective manner overall.

In my previous article, I highlighted a contrast between retail investor and institutional investor behavior, and judging from the above results, the conclusion drawn back then still applies. As shown above, for ETFs long-term, they saw net inflows of $132 billion, reflecting an LTM organic asset growth rate of 13%. This is an acceleration of growth from Q4, but on the other hand, institutional long-term saw net outflows overall. This may suggest that the 'smart money' is remaining cautious while retail investors have been a bit exuberant. Nonetheless, for BlackRock it seems that their iShares business is booming, and of course that is a great sign for the company as a whole.

Core Profitability Looking Good

In their Q1, BlackRock generated total revenues of $6.698 billion, up 27% YoY. This is a notable speedup in top line growth from Q4's 23% growth rate, and so business activity is heating up for the firm. Furthermore, they also beat analyst expectations by around $260 million, and so Q1 was a quarter of outperformance. In addition to the top line numbers, profitability also fared quite well.

For adjusted EPS, BlackRock saw an 11% increase, which is an acceleration from the previous quarter as well. While the bottom line grew slower than the top line, they were still able to surpass estimates by $1.03. In addition, operating margin is the better reflection of where the core profitability of the firm is at. In adjusted terms, operating margin expanded considerably from the prior year period's 43.2% to 44.5% to show that BlackRock is well run. Business efficiency continues to improve, and really, an operating margin in the 40s percentage range is very healthy.

Shareholders Enjoy Nice Capital Returns

With BlackRock's business booming, strong capital returns mean that shareholders get their share of the wealth. For their dividend, as previously promised, they have upped it to $5.73 per share, and as of now their yield is at an actually quite respectable 2.24%. In addition, after authorizing an additional 7 million shares for repurchase back in Q4, BlackRock upped their share repurchases YoY from $375 million to $450 million. These actions are obviously welcomed by investors, and in my view, they also demonstrate the continued confidence by the management team regarding their outlook.

Alternatives Push Continues

As stated in the introduction, BlackRock is pushing aggressively into alternatives. In their Q1, clients assets increased by 50% to $687 billion, while fee-paying AUM also increased by a very respectable 39% to $531 billion. These growth rates are nearly unchanged from the previous quarter, and so it is clear that the firm is continuing to push into this area. Alternatives have been historically a very lucrative space, and so their continued ambitions here could pay off in the long run.

At the end of March, it was reported that the Trump Administration is proposing to include alternatives in 401(k) plans. This could result in a significant reallocation of capital. While this could take away from some of BlackRock's other businesses, it could provide major tailwinds for their alternative efforts. If these new rules ultimately do get implemented, shareholders will look back on BlackRock's move to pursue this area as a key strategic decision.

Private Credit Risks

There is little question that the largest risk facing BlackRock today is their notable exposure to private credit. If you go and take a look back at the alternatives chart above, you can see that as of the end of Q1, they have $210 billion of client assets and $147 billion of fee-paying AUM in private credit. Therefore, if the crisis of confidence continues, BlackRock may see some effects.

Let's go over a timeline of how private credit risks have emerged at BlackRock. In early March, the company wrote down a second private loan to zero. To amplify the concerns, just a few months ago this loan was valued at 100 cents on the dollar. Such a rapid collapse in value shows that the private credit space likely has issues with mark-to-market policies. Just a day after this news broke out, BlackRock had to limit withdrawals from their private credit fund as a sign that there's a major lack of confidence. Despite these concerning signs, in reference to Mohamed El-Erian's comments about parallels to 2007, CEO Larry Fink stated that

I would love to debate Mohamed right now. I don’t see any similarities at all. Zero. 2007 was based on hidden leverage, just gigantic leverage balance sheets. This is not a leverage balance sheet problem.

Fink's views are also a stark contrast to comments that JPMorgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon made just quite recently, and so at this point, we really do not know how this crisis will play out. For BlackRock, this is nonetheless a notable risk that they have to deal with. Lastly, earlier this month, it was reported that lawmakers are probing private credit firms, including BlackRock, with regard to their private credit practices. This is a suggestion that actions may have been questionable.

Valuation Analysis

At the current reading of 20.64, the forward P/E ratio for BlackRock is below levels seen back in my previous update. With the valuation far below the highs seen last year, I believe the risk/reward remains net attractive for investors despite the private credit saga that's still playing out.

Today, it was shown that BlackRock is continuing to experience robust inflows as a sign that business activity has remained high. With LTM organic fee growth accelerating, their monetization of this activity is also improving. In terms of profitability, an expanding operating margin clearly shows that BlackRock is being run in a very efficient manner. Finally, their continued alternatives push is justified, especially given the Trump Administration's 401(k) proposal.

While the company does have a notable exposure to private credit, it should also be mentioned that their exposure isn't at terrifying levels. Relative to the entire firm's $13.89 trillion AUM at the end of Q1, private credit is a business that's unlikely to cause any kind of existential crisis for BlackRock, and so despite the concerning signs in that space, I believe the firm is still overall on solid footing.

With that in mind, I would say that the slight valuation contraction since my previous article presents investors with a chance to buy into this asset management giant. The private credit crisis and Iran war have seemingly kept a lid on the stock price, but given the current fundamental setup, I believe stock outperformance is likely moving forward.

Conclusion

BlackRock has experienced mild losses since my previous article, and while that was unexpected, I believe the stock is ready for outperformance after reporting solid Q1 numbers. As a whole, I remain impressed with their fundamental strength, especially given the macro and geopolitical environment. While private credit is indeed a risk worth monitoring, it is also a risk that has seemingly been somewhat overblown. While headlines of write downs and withdrawal limits have spooked some investors, their exposure to this space is not overly large considering their total AUM. With the valuation below where it was back in my previous update, I believe the risk/reward remains attractive enough to justify another reiteration of the buy rating.

data.title: Towering 10-Day A/D Lines
data.description:
The Iran war resulted in a tough March for US equities. In addition to the S&P 500 falling close to 10% versus its 52-week high, breadth took a measurable hit. As shown below, up until the first week of March, the 10-day advance/decline line, which measures the net share of index members rising or falling each day over ten days, was positive for most of the year.

Once equities began selling off, this measure tanked, and at the low on March 13th, it reached the lowest level since 12/19/24, which also ranks as a 2nd percentile reading going back to 1990.

With the benefit of hindsight, we can now say that low in breadth pre-dated the low in price (which came on March 30th). It has now been ten trading days since the low, and daily breadth has been positive all but twice (April 7th and April 10th were the only days with negative daily breadth). As a result, the 10-day A/D line is now at the highest levels since 7/10/25.

As we highlighted in yesterday's Sector Snapshot, not only has the S&P 500's 10-day A/D line risen sharply, but several other sectors have as well. As shown below, Consumer Discretionary, Industrials, and Real Estate all went from extreme oversold readings (2+ standard deviations below the historical average) to extreme overbought readings today. While the reading isn't quite as extreme, Tech has also seen a sharp rise in its line.

As might be evident from the one-year charts above, by far the most extended breadth measure comes out of the real estate sector. To give greater historical context, below we show the 10-day A/D line going back to September 2016, when it became a sector.

With another move higher today, real estate's 10-day A/D line is at the highest level since 2/2/23. The only other times the 10-day A/D line was more elevated were from January to February 2019 and in June 2020.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: A Closer Look At Full-Time And Part-Time Employment: March 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Buried near the bottom of table A-9 of the government's employment situation summary are the numbers for full- and part-time workers, with 35-or-more hours as the arbitrary divide between the two categories. The source is the monthly current population survey (CPS) of households. The focus is on total hours worked regardless of whether the hours are from a single or multiple jobs.

The Labor Department has been collecting this since 1968, a time when only 13.5% of US employees were part-timers. Over 55 years later, March's employment report showed that 17.4% of total employed workers were part time and 82.6% of total employed workers were full-time.

Here is a visualization of the trend in the 21st century, with the percentage of full-time employed on the left axis and the part-time employed on the right. We see a noticeable crossover during the Great Recession when part-time work increased, and full-time work declined. Over the past few years, we have seen a similar crossover slowly form.

The Impact of Recessions on Employment

Let's zoom in a bit closer to see the impact of the Great Recession on employment. We’ve changed the start date of our next chart to the beginning of 2007. The reversal began in 2008, but it accelerated in the fall of that year following the September 15th bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers.

In January 2010, shortly after the official end of the recession, part-time work peaked at 20.1%, while full-time work reached an all-time low of 79.9%. Afterwards, the two cohorts slowly drifted apart, with full-time employment increasing.

Interestingly, this trend continued even during the COVID global pandemic and recession. It was only until recently that we started to see a slight reversal in this trend, with full-time work slowly declining while part-time work is on the rise. In December 2023, full-time employment dropped below 83.0% of all employment while part-time employment rose above 17.0% and we have been there ever since.

Here's a longer-term view of this same chart. With the extended timeline, we can see a few more examples of what has typically happened to these two kinds of work during a recession.

You’ll notice that the recessions from the early 90s and early 2000s saw similar action as the Great Recession: Full-time work declined while part-time work rose. But again, this was not the case during the most recent recession in 2020.

In fact, full-time employment peaked in April 2020, possibly because of a shift in the type of full-time work available at the start of the pandemic. There were some employees who shifted to part-time from full-time as a result of the pandemic; however, it was only about 3% of those working part-time.

One more interesting feature in the chart above is the dramatic change in the full-time/part-time ratio at the beginning of 1994. In January 1994, the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) came into effect. This was a trade agreement between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico, aimed at reducing barriers to trade and investment among the three countries.

While NAFTA led to increased trade and economic growth overall, it also had various effects on employment. Some industries saw an increase in part-time work due to outsourcing or changes in demand, while others experienced a decline in full-time positions.

Let's have a closer look at why some employees work part-time, voluntarily and involuntarily:

A noteworthy trend we have seen over the past few years is the rise in part-time work for involuntary reasons.

A Closer Look at the Core Workforce, Ages 25-54

The two charts above are seasonally adjusted and include the entire workforce, which the CPS defines as age 16 and over. A problem inherent in using this broadest of cohorts is that it includes the population that adds substantial summertime volatility to the full-time/part-time ratio, namely, high school and college students.

Also, the 55-plus cohort includes a subset of employees that opt for part-time employment during the decade following the historical peak earning years (ages 45-54) and as a transition toward retirement.

The next chart better illustrates summertime volatility by focusing on the change since 2007 in full- and part-time employment for the 25-54 workforce. The government's full-time/part-time data for this cohort is only available as non-seasonally adjusted. To help us recognize the summer seasonality, the June-July-August markers are shown in a lighter color.

These months are more subject to temporary shifts from part-time to 35-plus hours of employment. The 12-month moving averages for the two series help us identify the slope of the trend in recent years. Again, we see that over the past few months the trend has reversed where part-time employment has increased while full-time employment has decreased.

In March 2026, part-time employment for the "core" workforce made up 11.3% of all "core" employment.

Like the 16-and-over version, this chart depicts a current situation that is considerably different from the era before the financial crisis, although the trend has been slowly improving since early 2010, as illustrated by the dotted-line moving averages. In this non-seasonally adjusted data, the summer months are obvious outliers, with full-time employment spiking during the June-August period.

Obamacare and Part-Time Employment

Many have speculated that the Affordable Care Act (aka Obamacare) has played a role in company decisions about full-time versus part-time employment. The $2,000 per employee penalty for companies that do not comply with regulations has influenced some to rethink their employment policies.

In July 2013, the government pushed the start of the penalty from January 2014 to January 2015. But the anticipation of the penalty, even though delayed a year, no doubt influenced the decisions of private employers.

With regard to Obamacare and part-time employment, the surge in part-time employment was triggered by the recession, not by the Affordable Care Act, as the next chart clearly illustrates.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics' monthly employment report is a hodgepodge of data. The full-time/part-time ratio is but a tiny piece of the whole, and the magic 35-hour threshold is arguably arbitrary. But this is, nevertheless, a metric that bears close watching as we try to understand where the economy is headed.

The question is whether the ratio shift is to some extent a structural change initially triggered by the great recession but now influenced by a combination of factors, not least of which are workplace demographics and ongoing increases in technology-driven productivity with less dependence on human workforce participation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: XSD In The Semiconductor Boom, But Not The Choice I Prefer. Here's Why
data.description:
I am very fascinated by SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) because it positions itself in a highly vertical market concentrated on a few stocks with an unusual approach: equal weight. An approach that brings added value to the portfolio? Although ideologically it seems like a real “breath of fresh air” to reduce excessive weights, such as those of names like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), my honest opinion is the opposite. In my view, doing so would distort the semiconductor market. A market that today, in my opinion, deserves particular attention, despite the conflict in Iran. But let me explain better…

What is XSD?

XSD is in all respects a pure sector index instrument on semiconductors, with systematic and optimized replication of the benchmark, the S&P Semiconductor Select Industry Index (a widely used index built with a modified equal-weighted methodology).

The expense ratio mentioned multiple times is 0.35%, barely covered by the dividend yield, which TTM is equal to 0.21% (which is natural considering it represents the quintessential US growth segment).

How is It Built?

The fund is naturally 100% exposed to semiconductors, with a focus on the US segment, and the most interesting and distinctive aspect is precisely that the benchmark is modified equal-weighted.

This introduces a clear economic effect, namely the strong reduction in the weight of mega-caps and the increase in the relative weight of mid- and small-caps. Not surprisingly, there are 45 holdings, and the top 10 account for less than 30% (unusual for this type of sector ETF).

It is interesting how, although it is still true that no single stock really dominates, the choice not to give weight to the most capitalized semiconductor companies, in my opinion, still leads to an imbalance. To be clear, in XSD tickers like Nvidia, which practically control the market, almost “disappear” from the radar, ceasing to be the dominant driver of sector performance. Good, you might think… yet the truth is that today, they are dominant drivers of the sector.

I think the fund can offer exposure to emerging or adjacent segments but also introduce complexity and volatility that are less immediately readable. And this is an element that clearly emerges when we examine the daily holding distribution shared by SPDR. In fact, about 78.8% of the weight in XSD is skewed toward segments below the large-cap category; those stocks that populate market-weight ETFs (names below $10B control 78.8% of XSD). Only 21% is covered by Nvidia, Broadcom, Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD), QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM), Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI), Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), and Intel Corporation (INTC).

Peer Analysis

And peer analysis becomes central in this context, and personally I think the comparison with the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), two market-weighted ETFs, is very interesting. These ETFs are much more concentrated in mega-leaders. Here is a comparison from SA data useful to understand the differences in the holding distribution.

And at this point, it is interesting to note how, by extending the time frame, market-weighted solutions have outperformed with the arrival of AI.

And the fundamental reason is only one: AI is extremely capital-intensive, and hyperscalers’ CapEx has gone directly to mega-caps.

Big Tech has announced an increase in CAPEX. The money goes to the big players in the semiconductor sector; in the AI ecosystem, there are always Nvidia, Broadcom, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), and Advanced Micro Devices. It does not go to Cirrus Logic, Inc. (CRUS) or SiTime Corporation (SITM). Why is that? Simply because in semiconductors the winning software architecture creates hardware lock-in. Nvidia’s CUDA is not just a GPU; it is an ecosystem of 15 years of libraries, tools, and accustomed developers. It is no coincidence that Nvidia has around 80% of the high-end AI data center chip market, with estimates above 90% in training workloads.

Let’s Narrow the Horizon From March

By narrowing the time horizon to 1M, we notice how, compared to the lows reached at the end of March, this segment is recovering across all 3 ETFs.

And this is normal; with the conflict in Iran, valuations, especially in the tech sector, have returned to the 10- and 30-year averages. But it is also true that Information Technology is the main driver of earnings growth for the S&P 500 in Q1 2026, and within IT the dominant contribution comes precisely from “Semiconductors & Semiconductor Equipment.” More specifically, FactSet writes that IT is expected to be at +45.0% YoY in earnings, the highest figure among all sectors, and that the Semiconductors & Semiconductor Equipment industry is the main contributor; without this group, IT earnings growth would drop to 20.1% from 45.0%. This comes in response to a net margin for Information Technology expected at 28.9% vs. 25.4% a year ago. Considering that CapEx has increased by 70% YoY, this would be a very important confirmation for this segment. And with the evolution of the conflict and Trump’s rhetoric shifting from truce to new blockades of the Strait of Hormuz, the market has started to understand Trump’s trade strategy and assign less weight to it on forward valuations (which had become very cheap).

My Opinion

And according to data updated as of April 10, they still are: the 12-month forward P/E of tech is at 22x, below the 5-year average and around the 10-year average.

If confirmed, this would make the upward CAPEX path sustainable, which would positively impact semiconductor guidance. Therefore, I am bullish in this direction on this segment.

Risk

However… specifically, I am not very bullish on XSD. This is precisely because the semiconductor sector seems to be increasingly taking on a vertical dimension toward a few players with distinctive MOATs. And an ETF that instead assigns weights diverging from those given by the market, in my opinion, will not bring added value. Considering that the contribution is not there statistically, even in terms of standard deviation and annualized volatility compared to other ETFs, I would prefer to use cap-weighted ETFs as a reference.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on this segment, but I think the right rating for XSD is HOLD. This is because, in my opinion, the equal weight formula poorly represents the (current) underlying structure of this market, and I do not see positive catalysts in this sense. That said, I remain bullish on the semiconductor segment: the market is assigning less weight to the impact of Trump’s geopolitical choices and has chosen to buy forward valuations at the 10-year average

data.title: SolarEdge Technologies: Up 200% In 12 Months And Room For More Ahead
data.description:
Company Background

SolarEdge Technologies, Inc. (SEDG) – founded in 2006 and based in Herzliya, Israel – has been one of the most successful turnaround stories of the past 12 months. In the lead-up to 2024/25, the company had an enormous glut of inventory from its declining European business line (up to $1.5 billion in value at its height, Q1 2024). SolarEdge had seen its share price fall over 90% from its peak in 2021, and the looming problems of tariffs from overseas manufacturing, falling demand globally due to elevated interest rates, and the threat of Biden-era subsidies being pulled sent many shareholders for the hills in late 2023/2024, as seen below.

The company embarked on a series of layoffs, reducing the workforce by around 25% throughout 2024. The inventory glut forced the company to write down almost half of its value in Q3 2024, incurring huge losses ($1.2 billion that quarter). A new CEO – Shuki Nir – took over in late 2024 and immediately set about refocusing efforts on U.S. domestic manufacturing, mainly in Texas, Florida, and Utah. He figured that, as Republican states, they would be better protected from any potential Biden-era policy roll-back that Trump might have initiated, and he was proved correct. The shift to domestic manufacturing allowed the company to enjoy tax credits from the Inflation Reduction Act, and, as interest rates began to come down, they responded to the improved demand among end users with strong revenue growth.

In 2025, revenue growth began to recover, as did the company’s gross margins. From being overall negative in 2024, SolarEdge now boasts a 22% gross margin in Q4 2025.

If we zoom into the last 12 months, therefore, SolarEdge has done phenomenally well, with shares more than tripling in value. Helping their cause still further was the agreement signed in November 2025 to assist Infineon with developing a solid-state transformer for AI data centers. This positions the company as a key beneficiary – a picks and shovels play – for the data center build-out program over the following years.

With the stock’s strong performance over the previous twelve months, investors will want to know what upside is left and what of its risk-reward profile. Below, I’ll assess the broader industry dynamics as I see them and provide a snapshot of SolarEdge’s moat, if applicable. I’ll also assess its financials and valuation metrics.

Recovery Within the Solar Industry

The solar industry is highly cyclical, with boom-and-bust periods characterizing its long history. We are arguably at a low point currently, but sentiment is improving, and we may be entering a period of growth as the data center build-out takes precedence as an industrial necessity. The demand for cheap energy is only expected to continue rising, and solar remains one of the most likely beneficiaries. According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration (seen below), solar installations will represent the largest proportion of new energy installations in 2026 (more than 51%).

Further, the trendline for solar going forward continues to suggest a broader recovery for the industry. By the end of 2027, almost 10% of all energy generated is expected to be from solar power, with wind the only other energy source seeing an increase in build-out. This is despite all the noise coming out about “drill, baby, drill.” Behind the scenes, the superior practicality, feasibility, and unit economics of solar are trumping any politicized preference for alternatives, especially fossil fuels. And while nuclear power gets much of the attention, those who are looking 1-2 years ahead instead of 10-20 realize that only solar can be deployed within such timeframes to satisfy demand levels that are rapidly reaching “immediate.”

I see no reason why this period of increasing demand will not continue going forward. As such, I remain as bullish as ever on the solar industry and view companies like SolarEdge as beneficiaries of this long-term durable trend. It seems the investment community has already come around to this. Judging from the performance of some of the leading publicly listed solar companies – including FirstSolar (FSLR), Enphase (ENPH), Sunrun (RUN), Array Technologies (ARRY), and NextPower (NXT) – the industry has begun to rebound.

All except Enphase have recovered by a considerable margin, with SolarEdge outperforming the lot. Much of this has to do with Enphase’s sustained reliance on European revenue, which has been poor, and with Chinese-sourced battery cells, which the company has been slow to phase away from. In time, I suspect they will also have their moment of recovery, but the past 12 months have proven what strong execution on a plan can bring a company in turmoil. Let’s examine where SolarEdge is positioned in the solar market generally before assessing their moat.

SolarEdge: Assessing Their Market Position

SolarEdge offers a host of products in both residential and commercial power markets, which are their main markets.

Most of their revenue is derived from selling power inverters and optimizers alongside batteries for photovoltaic (PV) (solar panel) applications. They are one of the leading companies in the U.S. solar market, boasting in December 2025 via a promotional video to have returned as #1 in rooftop solar, #1 in the U.S. residential solar market share, and #1 in U.S. commercial solar market share. In 2025, 60.9% of their revenue was derived from the US (26.9% from Europe; 12.6% from RoW), and revenue jumped 44% despite a challenging global market.

For SolarEdge, their revenue is wrapped around the key components involved in rooftop solar: the inverter, power optimizer, and battery. Let’s start with the inverter. This is the system that converts the electricity produced from a solar panel (direct current, or DC) into electricity that can be used in a home or power grid (alternating current, or AC). As I understand it, there are typically two main types of inverter: a string inverter and a microinverter. SolarEdge specializes in the former.

In a string converter system, all of the panels are linked together in a series, with the DC energy flowing into a single, centralized inverter. This one box converts all the electricity into AC. The benefit of this system is that it offers relatively cheap setup and is best for simple solar systems where consistent sunlight with no shading or soiling/dirt is expected. This is important because the string converter system runs like Christmas tree lights, where lower performance in one section has a ripple effect for the entire system. So, shading on one panel can lower the total energy produced across the gamut. Essentially, the entire system’s performance is determined by the lowest-producing panel.

This system differs from the more advanced microinverter system sold by competitors like Enphase, which has mini converter systems attached to each panel separately, meaning minimal energy disruption caused by shading or soiling. This system is better for complex roofs with some shading/soiling expected or where users may want to monitor individual panels or expand the system with additional panels at some later point. With these advantages come high upfront costs but typically longer warranties/guarantees on product quality and durability.

SolarEdge has attempted to overcome the deficiencies in the traditional string inverter systems by fitting each panel with a power optimizer system. This aims to squeeze the most power out of each panel and ensure that no power is lost across the entire series. Shade or dirt on one panel won’t detract from the performance of the whole system as it would through a traditional string inverter. The SolarEdge power optimizer system employs MPPT (maximum PowerPoint tracking) to monitor conditions and find the best voltage and current system at each moment and for each panel. A change in sunlight, shading, or temperature will impact energy production, but the system finds the “sweet spot.” This enables it to compete with Enphase (e.g., its popular IQ8 system) in terms of performance, though there is still the problem of SolarEdge having a single point of failure in its inverter system. By relying on a single central inverter, if it fails, the whole system gets shut down until it can be replaced/fixed. Through the MySolarEdge app, users can fully track the performance of their systems.

Tesla (TSLA) is also a competitor in this space. Its inverters bring a similar level of performance (over 97% efficiency), and they offer advanced monitoring technology through the Tesla app. The Tesla Powerwall 3 system offers energy storage as part of the whole system, providing energy backup in case of electricity power outages.

SolarEdge has also ventured into the battery and energy storage markets and has achieved some success in terms of product deployment over the past two years. As we can see below, the revenue from batteries in Q4 2025 has become a major component of their total revenues. We can also see the winding down of their energy storage business, which they shut down. They had initially acquired the South Korean firm Kokam in 2018 but closed this division in late 2024 to focus on their core residential solar market, including battery solutions. This was part of the company’s strategic restructuring.

SolarEdge: Assessing Their Moat

SolarEdge has patents protecting their proprietary inverter technology, which provides something of a moat. However, I do not necessarily view it as superior to Enphase. In fact, it seems many in the market prefer Enphase products based on what I can see from reviews. However, in residential and commercial solar, switching costs are high, which means that customers who purchased an array are unlikely to rip it up and replace it with an entirely new system unless they have had a terrible experience with it. Thus, SolarEdge’s installed base should bring it a small amount of recurring revenue going forward from its cloud-based monitoring platform and SolarEdge One, its AI-based personal energy assistant.

However, as a hardware company, mainly, they do not have a rock-solid moat that is impenetrable within the marketplace. They have good products, and when demand is high, they will perform well as a company. Investors should understand the cyclicality of this industry and recognize that probably the most important factor is market share. This is what will determine revenue growth during market upswings and protect them during inevitable downturns. Let’s compare their market share to their most direct competitor, Enphase.

In 2025, SolarEdge saw enormous market growth with its inverters and power optimizers, leading to 89.2% U.S. revenue growth for the year. This compares to relatively flat year-over-year microinverters shipped and megawatts DC shipped for Enphase and just 27.3% U.S. revenue growth. It seems that for Enphase, their declining European business really dragged on the stronger growth they enjoyed in the U.S. as the industry recovers. SolarEdge therefore saw a sustained market share gain in this region. If we compare global deployment of Enphase microinverters versus SolarEdge power optimizers, in 2024 they were nearly equal, whereas in 2025, they took an enormous jump forward. Evidently, SolarEdge positioned themselves much better during the bottom-out phase before the market began to recover.

Enphase saw strong growth in its IQ batteries, but this was not enough to offset decline in other parts of their business. Meanwhile, SolarEdge also likely took share from Tesla Powerwall. In its latest 10-K, Tesla declared 2025 revenue from its energy generation and storage segment as $8.969 billion versus $7.446 billion the previous year. While this positions their energy storage business as their fastest-growing segment, it is unclear how much of this was related to their PowerWall (residential clients) versus MegaPack (electricity grid operators and industrial clients) revenue, or how much of this was derived from the U.S. versus internationally. However, it would not be unreasonable to assume that the growth rate was less than 89%, meaning they are losing market share to SolarEdge too.

So, while SolarEdge achieved market share gains in several areas and returned to FCF positive in 2025 ($81 million versus $421 million cash burn in 2024), they are still not profitable. However, it is possible they achieve profitability within the next 12 months. Enphase, meanwhile, has remained profitable throughout the entire industry downturn. They also have much better margins across the board, combining the last 4 quarters.

So, while SolarEdge has had stronger revenue growth, they are still paying for this growth with debt, stock dilution, interest payments, and, until recently, cash burn. In spite of this, SolarEdge investors have been buying stock in anticipation of sustained growth. However, I do not necessarily see SolarEdge as having a durable competitive advantage beyond their technology that is not necessarily superior to their chief rival’s and is fit for only certain conditions. As the leading player in string inverters, their business success will depend upon sustained demand for this type of inverter, but as solar installations are increasingly built on rooftops in non-traditional spaces (i.e., some shading or in complex environments), this will likely place greater demand on the microinverter systems over the string inverter systems.

I feel there will always be a market for a cheaper, less advanced alternative product line for those seeking to deploy solar, but I suspect SolarEdge’s recent performance has as much to do with Enphase stumbling than SolarEdge and its technology/product line having a kind of edge. As they mainly sell to new customers, the stickiness of their product within the market is called into question. It is true that those who install these systems might have preferences and then pass on their preferences when customers ask for build-out advice, but ultimately they will go where the business is, and if customers come to demand the more advanced Enphase IQ technology that comes with a 15-year warranty over SolarEdge’s 10- or 12-year warranty, the installers will have to adjust their preferences.

That said, it is unclear at this stage how knowledgeable their customer base is with regard to the pros and cons of the different systems. I note that SolarEdge recently released a new product line in Germany, their SolarEdge Nexis system that is a kind of plug-and-play system for residential solar + storage.

This allows it to compete more closely against the Tesla Powerwall and Enphase IQ 5P and meet new customer preferences. As the industry moves in this general direction, with home energy storage becoming a more common and sought-after feature, it is possible, if not likely, that all 3 of these companies (among others) can win, and the industry may remain fragmented until one of them delivers a truly exceptional product that distinguishes them from their competitors. At this point, I do not see it. Tesla may be the only one with something resembling a moat, but much of this is tied to how their energy storage systems integrate with their vehicles, and their vehicle sales have been declining for two years now. Tesla, meanwhile, is in a much stronger position in terms of having capital to deploy to R&D, though I note SolarEdge invested $221 million in R&D in 2025, compared to $189 million for Enphase.

I would assign SolarEdge a no or low moat status, as I do not believe they necessarily have any kind of unassailable position of dominance in this industry or a durable advantage that their competitors could not also seize upon. Given their higher (and improving) margins, it would appear that Enphase enjoys some benefit related to economies of scale on the production/distribution side, but SolarEdge still has to prove itself in this regard. They are on pace to record improved margins, but will they touch those of Enphase? At this point it is unclear, making Enphase probably the safer long-term bet.

The nature of the solar business is that they must always seek out new customers or develop relationships with suppliers to expand their customer base, and their new customers are not tied to any particular type of technology. They could choose Enphase as much as they could choose SolarEdge, so what is driving the business generally is the broad-based shift to renewable energy and solar as a driving force of this energy transition.

Let’s look at their financials and valuation metrics before assessing the risk/reward profile of the company right now.

Financials and Valuation Metrics

SolarEdge has cash and equivalents of $493 million amidst current assets valued at $1.74 billion. Inventory is valued at $552.6 million, which is still really high, given its inventory turnover is just 0.44. For comparison, Enphase has an inventory turnover score of 3.5 presently. Last year, SolarEdge generated less revenue than the total value of their inventory; Enphase generated 5x their inventory value figure.

SolarEdge has a total debt of $386.77 million, with almost all of it ($331.6 million) classified as long-term debt. This gives SolarEdge a net cash position of $106 million. In the last two years, shareholders equity (book value) fell from $2.4 billion to $427 million, which would have stung investors. Management has worked over this time to pay off some of its debt, repaying $347 million in 2025 alone. According to a recent 8-K, the company’s remaining debt obligation is due to be repaid in 2029.

Their debt/equity score of 0.9 is too high for my liking and suggests some risk. The company has a quick ratio of 0.95 and a current ratio of 2.17. Presently, their cash is not enough to cover their current liabilities.

As the company has navigated this difficult previous period, the company has issued stock, and shareholders have been diluted somewhat, approximately 13% over the last 5 years. As they are now generating FCF, I would not expect much more dilution in the next 12 months. In terms of cash generation, management seems capable of reaching a sufficient level to repay their debt due in 2029, though it is certainly possible that more debt is taken on in 2027 or 2028 or their 2029 obligation is refinanced at a better interest rate. This is something investors should keep an eye on. The company needs to work on improving its balance sheet.

Management guided in the last call for $305 million revenue at the midpoint for Q1 2026, which would represent 20-24% non-GAAP gross margins and potentially reaching EBIT profitability later in the year.

In terms of valuation, this is a difficult company to value due to the cyclicality of the industry it is in and because it does not currently generate a profit. On a P/S ratio basis, it carries a score above 2 and an EV/sales ratio of 2.1. These are within their 5-year historical range but certainly high compared to last year.

It has a P/B of 6.1, according to Seeking Alpha, which gives it a D+ rating on valuation overall.

Risks and Concluding Thoughts

SolarEdge has performed exceptionally well in this challenging macro environment for solar energy in 2025, but much of its success must be understood in the context of easy comparisons to 2024’s dire numbers. Management is expecting the launch of its Nexis product in Germany to represent a major step forward, but with Europe still struggling with oversupply and an inventory glut, their success remains to be seen. In the U.S., state-wide changes to net-metering credits in California have continued to stifle demand, while pushback against Israeli business in the U.S. due to geopolitical developments could remain a factor. Having said that, management seemed ahead of the game in their quick pivot to U.S.-based manufacturing—a move that has shielded them somewhat from the worst of the tariffs.

I have confidence in Nir as a leader and would expect SolarEdge to continue performing well as the broader need for solar energy shows no sign of abating. Despite the headwinds, I view the sustained push into solar energy as a clear indication of its superior unit economics becoming recognized. As solar comes to take a larger place in the energy mix going forward, SolarEdge will continue to benefit. Its moves into solar as part of a broader home energy packaging system place it at the forefront of modern technology and consumer preferences. Despite my reservations related to balance sheet risk and lack of a clear, definitive moat to shield it from competitors, I rate shares a Buy.

data.title: Kraft Heinz Vs. Mondelez: Same Roots, Diverging Trajectories
data.description:
Intro

Having done quite a few food‑stock analyses lately in search of potentially undeservedly beaten‑down names, I couldn’t leave The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) untouched. Once considered a symbol of a rock‑solid defensive name, it has struggled for almost a decade up to this day.

Looking further into the Kraft Heinz story, I discovered it was actually spun off from its parent company, Kraft Foods, in 2012. Following the split, Kraft Foods was renamed Mondelez International, Inc. (MDLZ), the well-known global leader in confectionery and snacks.

When I compared the two stock charts, the difference was striking. Let’s just say the chocolate and snacks business has performed far better than the remaining packaged‑grocery segment.

Shareholders of Kraft’s former parent company who held onto Mondelez have been rewarded very well, while the KHC shares (formerly Kraft Foods Group before its merger with Heinz) that they received as dividends have strongly underperformed.

Shortly, over the last 15 years, Mondelez has delivered a 177% return to its shareholders, while Kraft Heinz has reduced investors' capital by roughly 18%, even after accounting for dividends.

When I look at that chart, I can’t help wondering whether now might be the moment when, say, over the next 15 years, KHC could actually outperform Mondelez. At first glance, it doesn’t seem utopian at all. KHC appears to have adopted a strong “whatever it takes” approach, aligning all its efforts to adapt to the changing marketplace and get the business back into better shape, with a new CEO at the helm as well. At the same time, MDLZ operates in a category that is widely acknowledged as being among the most challenged currently, as consumers shift toward healthier and lower‑carb options.

That said, Mondelez's stock has been so resilient that the market now values it at $74 billion versus KHC’s $27 billion. For context, the two generate revenues of $38.5 billion and $25 billion, respectively. It means the market values Mondelez's one revenue dollar roughly two times more than Kraft's.

It’s fair to say the market hasn’t even started factoring in Mondelez’s need to strongly adapt to marketplace changes. A brief look at both companies’ P/E ratios, based on this year's estimates, shows a multiple of 11 for KHC and 19 for Mondelez. It almost feels like the snacking and confectionery giant is operating on a different planet, untouched by the food industry selloff happening on Earth.

These were my initial thoughts when I decided to compare the two head-to-head to better understand why the market values them so differently and to see which might offer better returns for investors over the next 15 years.

Kraft Heinz In Brief

History

While Mondelez and Kraft Heinz share the same early roots—going back to the founding of J.L. Kraft & Bros. Company in 1909 by James L. Kraft—Kraft's modern history effectively begins in 2012, when the businesses were split. The new company became Kraft Foods Group and was spun off, while the original global snacks division was renamed Mondelez International.

In 2015, Kraft merged with Heinz, a company founded by Henry J. Heinz, who started his first business in the 1870s.

Today, Kraft Heinz has approximately 35,000 employees globally based in 40 countries. It has two headquarters, one in Chicago and one in Pennsylvania. The first was the original headquarters of Kraft Foods, and the second is where Heinz has traditionally had its main office.

The company is among the world’s top 10 largest food producers and 3rd in the U.S. after Tyson Foods (TSN) and Mondelez.

Latest Results

The latest FY25 earnings report showed declines across all metrics, except for free cash flow.

Looking further behind the numbers, North America was the weakest performer, while developed markets actually grew.

The latest quarter beat expectations on the bottom line but slightly missed on the top line.

Outlook

The revised 2026 profit forecast of $1.98 to $2.10 per share fell short of the Street’s $2.49 estimate.

Despite mixed earnings, a weak outlook, and the unexpected decision to cancel the company’s separation plan, the stock managed to stay fairly steady for weeks around the $25 support level. But after drifting sideways for a while following the news, it eventually took a sharp drop and is now down about 11% as of this writing.

Mondelez In Brief

As mentioned in the KHC brief, the companies’ shared journey traces its roots back to 1909.

Today, Mondelez employs 91,000 people and is headquartered in Chicago. Here’s how they present their business:

Mondelēz International empowers people to snack right in over 150 countries around the world. We're leading the future of snacking with iconic global and local brands such as Oreo, Ritz, LU, Clif Bar and Tate's Bake Shop biscuits and baked snacks, as well as Cadbury Dairy Milk, Milka and Toblerone chocolate.

We are one of the largest snack companies in the world with global net revenues of approximately $36 billion in 2024. We hold the #1 global position in biscuits (cookies and crackers) and #2 in chocolate, while we’re growing rapidly in baked snacks. We also make and sell gum & candy as well as various cheese & grocery and powdered beverage products in certain markets.

Latest Earnings

The earnings were strong, but as shown in the presentation, they were driven by significant price increases that offset volume declines, which actually accelerated in Q4 to 4.8% from the year’s average of 3.7%.

Chocolate was the top performer with 11.4% growth but at the same time saw a significant 9.4% drop in volumes in Q4, accelerating from the annualized 7.5% decline. From a volume perspective, the best performer was Gum & Candy, which fell by just 0.7% year-over-year in Q4.

The quarterly results beat expectations on both the top and bottom lines:

Outlook

MDLZ is targeting 3-5% long-term top-line growth, but 2026 projections point to weaker performance—ranging from flat to slightly positive—while free cash flow is expected to remain steady at 2025 levels.

The stock has traded mostly sideways since the earnings release, dipping about 3% from the release date to today:

Head-To-Head Comparison

Kraft Heinz Mondelez
Key risks The portfolio is not keeping pace with customers’ changing habits, and sales are declining consistently The categories they operate in are among the most negatively affected by shifting consumer eating habits
Opportunities With a new CEO in place, the company is accelerating efforts to regain profitable growth Further leverage strong pricing power and adjust the portfolio to better align with market trends. Chocolate input prices are declining, creating potential profitability upside
Cyclicality dependence low low to medium
Current FY Revenue Growth decline 1.5% to 3.5% growth 0 to 2%
2027-2028 annualized revenue growth flat to 1% 3%
Current FY EPS Growth decline 22% growth 3.7%
2027-2028 EPS growth 5% Average growth 9.3%
EBITDA margin 2025 23% 13%
EV/EBITDA FWD 8.9 13.9
Free Cash Flow TTM/Dividend 193% 130%
Net Debt/EBITDA 3 3.82
Projected share repurchase No exact figure has been provided, but they have maintained their commitment to shareholder returns. In 2025, buybacks totaled $436 million. Guided $2 billion
Dividend safety high medium to high

Summary & Conclusions

To sum up, in my view, both companies have quite a challenging road ahead when it comes to adapting their portfolios. KHC still needs to show it can deliver at least flattish top-line performance, while MDLZ must find a way to slow the volume decline, which cannot be offset by price increases indefinitely.

Mondelez probably has some pricing power left, and the decline in cocoa prices provides another opportunity for profitability leverage. KHC shareholders will have to place their hopes on the new CEO to stop the sales decline.

MDLZ, meanwhile, has somewhat higher exposure to commodity cyclicality, as cocoa and sugar are key inputs. That said, their pricing power offsets this reasonably well, as previously seen.

Mondelez is showing stronger growth metrics, while KHC is likely to see only minimal bottom-line improvement in the coming years.

KHC’s core profitability profile is stronger, with an EBITDA margin as high as 22% versus Mondelez at 13%.

KHC also trades at a more attractive EV/EBITDA multiple, though MDLZ arguably deserves a higher multiple given its growth outlook.

In terms of dividend safety, KHC is the clear winner. Mondelez’s leverage ratio has increased, and the company will need to manage its finances very carefully to maintain investment‑grade status while supporting both the dividend and substantial buybacks.

I rate both companies as Hold, but my pick is KHC. It’s trading at low multiples and offers a solid, well-covered 7% dividend. Moreover, if its sales decline stabilizes over the next few quarters, I will seriously consider an upgrade.

Mondelez, with a P/E of 19 and EV/EBITDA of 13.9, looks fully priced in my view. Given the category it operates in is among the most challenged considering GLP-1 headwinds, the HOLD is more of a neutral stance, as Mondelez shareholders seem as resilient to higher prices as its confectionery and snack buyers.

data.title: Smart Hedge Trades For A Fragile Ceasefire
data.description:
The Iran ceasefire is technically still in place, but barely. The first round of peace talks ended without agreement, the White House has threatened a naval blockade of Iranian shipping (and tolled) traffic, and President Trump said Iranian ships impeding US warships would be eliminated. The ceasefire has not collapsed, but it is hard to find a silver lining as things currently stand.

We started writing this piece last week, positioning for a low-probability breakdown hedge that the market was ignoring. The probability is no longer low. The ceasefire is fragile enough that a single headline can swing oil six percent in either direction, and the market is struggling to price a wide distribution of outcomes. We decided to put together a menu of hedge trades, five longs and five shorts, for a potential ceasefire breakdown.

Traditional wisdom during previous Middle East flare-ups has been to go long defense, and short airlines. In our view, there is limited alpha in these trades. We are looking for derivative positions that the market has not fully priced-in.

Where Things Stand

The ceasefire, which took effect on April 8th, is still nominally holding, but the diplomatic route has been erratic. US-Iran talks over the weekend ended without agreement, and both sides are back in their respective corners. The White House has openly threatened a Hormuz blockade while minesweeping activities were conducted, and the US military has said it can blockade Iranian ports if ordered. When the market first digested the threat, WTI spiked above $100. It has since pulled back to the high $80s on the White House floating renewed peace talks. The structural case does not change on a single headline.

Despite this, the ceasefire terms themselves were always narrow. They address direct state-on-state strikes and leave gaps for proxy activity in Iraq, Syria, Yemen, and Lebanon. There is nothing enforceable on Iran’s nuclear timeline. Stars and Stripes ran a headline yesterday characterizing the ceasefire as “hang[ing] by a thread” while the blockade took effect. Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu told reporters that the ceasefire "could end at any moment" and that Israel backs the blockade. CBS reported that the US Navy has begun its blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz. Those are not the headlines of a stable ceasefire.

Crude came full circle, from $104 during the opening week of the war back to the $80s on ceasefire hopes, shot back above $100 on blockade headlines, and now sits near $88 on new peace talks. The market is oscillating between two scenarios: a long-term ceasefire and renewed conflict. Our base case assumes the status quo sustains long enough for the hedges to pay out, even if the ceasefire ultimately holds.

Longs: Tankers, LNG, Fertilizer, and Drones

International Seaways (INSW)

Frontline (FRO) is the largest listed crude tanker operator. This company has the most press coverage, and it is the ticker that retail traders often pair with Brent momentum. At around $35 with a ~21x P/E, it is also too crowded and leaves little room at current valuations.

International Seaways is the better version of the same trade. At around $75, the stock trades at a roughly 12x trailing multiple, nearly a 40% discount to Frontline on earnings. Market cap is ~$3.7 billion against Frontline’s ~$7.9 billion, which means less sell-side coverage and passive-fund ownership. The fleet mix is what matters if Hormuz disruption moves from threat to reality: a blended inventory of VLCCs, Suezmaxes, and LR1s. When the strait is facing blockades, you do not just want crude tankers. You want the full logistics stack, and INSW is a one-stop shop.

INSW trades near its 52-week high of $78.51, so it’s not completely overlooked. But at a 12x multiple, there is enough meat on the bone to make INSW a sufficient tail-risk hedge. If the ceasefire stabilizes and tensions ease, the stock probably drifts back toward $65. If the ceasefire fails and disruption increases, $100 is easily achievable.

Scorpio Tankers (STNG)

Scorpio Tankers is the world’s largest owner/operator of product tankers, focused exclusively on transporting refined petroleum products like gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel rather than unrefined crude. STNG is the cheapest of the major listed tanker stocks on a P/E basis. The current price is ~$73, the trailing P/E ~11x, market cap is ~$3.96 billion, and the dividend yield is 2.35%.

With Hormuz disruption, the arbitrage that matters is not just crude oil, but refined product flows from Gulf refineries to Europe and Asia. This routing lengthens voyages and compresses global tanker supply. The rate spike for product tankers in a Hormuz scenario is typically larger in percentage terms than the crude tanker spike, because the fleets are smaller and more geographically concentrated.

STNG has cut leverage drastically in recent years. Their balance sheet is the strongest it’s been in a decade. For a hedge trade, STNG offers the best blend of valuation, balance sheet strength, and upside among the tanker group. Pairing it with INSW gives full petroleum-refined product coverage.

Flex LNG (FLNG)

Our LNG pick is still not on any Iran-war trade list we have seen. Flex LNG trades at around $30, with a market cap of nearly $1.6 billion, and delivers a 10% dividend yield.

Qatar is the world’s second-largest LNG exporter and ships its entire output through the Persian Gulf. Hormuz disruption does not just affect crude. It disrupts LNG flows to Europe and Asia at a moment when European inventories are lean, and the EU’s pivot away from Russian gas has made Gulf LNG increasingly important. Flex LNG operates a fleet of MEGI and XDF carriers on long-term charters. The company delivers a strong dividend, and investors should not be worried about short-term yield changes. With its existing long-term contracts, the company is locked into favorable terms, allowing investors to earn yield while the geopolitical wave passes.

If the ceasefire holds and tensions calm, the 10% yield pays you to wait on a conservatively financed shipping stock. If the ceasefire fails and Gulf LNG flows are disrupted, charter rates spike, and FLNG sees further upside on charter renewals. Flex is a trade to consider for investors looking for sector trades that deliver yield, despite intraday swings.

CF Industries (CF)

CF Industries is a nitrogen-based fertilizer producer, trading around $117 with a ~13x P/E and a ~$18 billion market cap. Fertilizer stocks are often overlooked in this conflict. In our view, two things are worth considering. First, natural gas is the primary input expense for nitrogen fertilizer, and Gulf LNG disruption has pushed prices up. That hits EU and Asian producers harder than it hits CF, which sources from cheap North American gas. CF’s margin spread over the global cost curve widens. Second, a Middle East oil shock historically drives inflation across commodities, and fertilizer prices tend to move with grain prices.

CF has traded between $70 and $140 for two years as revenue stagnated and the urea market remained oversupplied. At 13.6x P/E, the stock is priced moderately. An oil shock breaks the range. Even without one, you are paying a fair multiple for a well-capitalized, gas-advantaged producer with embedded tail-risk optionality. With WTI near $88, that protection is starting to look more real.

Kratos Defense (KTOS)

We are not recommending Lockheed or Northrop because too many people are already in those trades. Instead, Kratos Defense is our contrarian pick. The stock is down 45% from its 52-week high of $134.00 and trades at ~$72, with a market cap of nearly $14 billion.

Kratos makes drones, tactical unmanned aerial systems, loitering munitions, and other low-cost precision systems. These are the products that would be needed if the ceasefire fully breaks down. Enforcing a blockade in Hormuz is not an F-35-type mission. It is swarms of surveillance drones, counter-UAS defense, and loitering munitions to protect shipping lanes. That is what Kratos specializes in, not Lockheed.

Kratos’s P/E is distorted by heavy investment in the XQ-58A Valkyrie program and depressed trailing earnings. The stock sold off on the original ceasefire news, but in our view, that works to our benefit. We believe the dip creates an entry that looks better by the day. If things stabilize, KTOS likely grinds toward $80 on existing momentum. If the ceasefire fails, the stock likely moves toward the old highs on fundamentals and short covering. The stock is up from $70 last week, but still has ~$60 of upside between current prices and the 52-week high.

Shorts: Airlines, Cruise, Luxury, Homebuilders, Trucking

Delta Air Lines (DAL)

Delta is the cleanest airline short in any oil shock because it does not hedge against jet fuel spikes the way most peers do, which makes its earnings more sensitive to crude prices. Its Monroe Energy refinery "natural hedge" does not fully offset crude spikes, so fuel cost moves hit earnings faster than at peers. Currently trading at ~$70, representing a ~9.8x P/E and a ~$44 billion market cap. In our view, shorting a weaker airline brings additional risk layers that muddy the waters.

DAL is up from $67.82 last week and jumped today on renewed-talks headlines. In our view, that makes the entry point better, not worse. Airlines priced a Hormuz reopening before it happened. If WTI spikes back above $95, DAL likely gives most of these gains back. Long-dated puts still offer better leverage than outright shorts and help traders avoid short-term squeezes on diplomatic machinations.

Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH)

The cruise short in an oil shock is Norwegian Cruise Line, trading at ~$21, with a ~23x P/E and a market cap of ~$9.2 billion. Fuel is always at the top of cruise line expenditures, and Norwegian is already highly leveraged. As demand pulls back in the current economic climate, the balance sheet has not recovered enough to absorb a concurrent oil shock.

Norwegian’s balance sheet is the worst among cruise majors. Long-term debt remains high compared to pre-COVID levels, and free cash flow is not enough to delever without revenue growth. The war, subsequent oil spikes, and weaker consumer demand across the Eastern Mediterranean are bad signs for Norwegian.

Norwegian is close to its 52-week low of $15.31, so some risk is priced in. But with oil prices rising and the ceasefire weakening, the next drop could happen faster than the market expects.

LVMH ADR (LVMUY)

LVMH, trading back at ~$113, is the hardest call on this list, and we want to be upfront about that. The stock is now just 7% above its 52-week low of $101.80, and far below its high of $152.95. The slowdown in China’s luxury market had already hurt the stock, and now the worsening ceasefire is making things more difficult.

Luxury is the cleanest second-derivative play on an EU consumer slump. Sustained Gulf oil shortages would renew EU inflation fears and compound the slowdown in China’s economy. If the ceasefire stabilizes, LVMH probably finds a floor around current prices. If it fails and oil stays above $90, the stock is likely heading below $100 as input costs rise and consumer demand deteriorates. Despite this, in our view, LVMH is not a clean short at these levels. It is a position that gets worse in a tail scenario, which is exactly the kind of exposure to fade in a hedge book.

DR Horton (DHI)

Here is another short that is not getting much attention. DR Horton is at ~$145, with a ~13 P/E and a nearly $42 billion market cap. The stock is down from its 52-week high but is still trading at high multiples compared to peer homebuilders. Its price depends on the market’s belief that the Fed will cut rates, a thesis unlikely to play out if the oil shock continues.

Higher oil prices drive inflation, widen breakevens, and decrease the likelihood of a Fed rate cut. DHI is the largest and most liquid company in its peer group and has the tightest gross margin sensitivity because of its dependence on mortgage buydowns to clear inventory.

With WTI back near $88 and Hormuz rhetoric escalating, the rate-cut narrative is under significant pressure. Homebuilders are not top of mind in geopolitics, which is the whole reason we like the setup. If the ceasefire holds and inflation keeps cooling, DHI is fine. If it fails, DHI has direct exposure to the monetary policy consequences of an oil-driven inflation shock.

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL)

Old Dominion Freight Line is at ~$209, with a ~$44 billion market cap and a ~43 P/E. It is trading within 5% of its 52-week high of $221.63. In our view, this is the most important short on the list.

ODFL is the most efficient LTL carrier in North America. Despite the operational integrity, the base case is simple: at nearly 43x earnings, ODFL is priced for a soft landing, and the cost structure is directly exposed to diesel prices at a moment when oil swings wildly each session. The multiple compresses when rates move against you, and the operating margin compresses when diesel rises before contract pricing can reset.

For a ceasefire-failure hedge, a premium-multiple stock trading near all-time highs with direct commodity cost exposure is the kind of short that delivers outsized returns. If the ceasefire stabilizes, ODFL grinds higher and the hedge costs little. If the ceasefire fails, ODFL is an asymmetric trade. We do not say that lightly, but valuation and exposure are what they are.

Risks

First, there could be a diplomatic breakthrough. The first round of talks has broken down, but they could start again. If the White House backs away from talk of a blockade and a new agreement covers proxy activity and nuclear inspections, most of these trades would reverse. Tanker and LNG stocks would lose their gains, the defense pick would go sideways, and the shorts would lose value as risk premiums fade. We think this is less likely now than last week, but it is still possible. Position sizes should reflect that.

Second, Saudi Arabia and the UAE could decide to fully make up for Iranian supply with their own production. OPEC+ has plenty of spare capacity, and if they act together, it would limit price volatility, though logistics would be the one wrench in the gears. For an oil shock to linger, there needs to be a supply disruption and no way to replace it. LNG and product tankers are less affected by this risk than crude tankers, which is why FLNG and STNG are our top long picks.

What To Watch

We find a helpful resource is reviewing crude tanker charter rates on the Ras Tanura to Ningbo route. These are updated weekly and usually move before stock prices when the market senses disruption risk. Another is the skew on long-dated Brent call options. If implied volatility on out-of-the-money Brent calls stays high, even as spot prices fall, the derivatives market is signaling long-term disruption risk.

The market is swinging violently between complacency and panic on every diplomatic development. This type of volatility can yield great returns for discerning investors, while everyone else chases the last headline.
