data.title: 3i Group: 18.5x Could Be Too High A Multiple For Action
data.description:
3i Group plc (TGOPF) is not our favorite. The majority of its NAV is dictated by Action, whose valuation is too high in our view relative to other European peers that are growing profit at similar rates. Moreover, we generally question whether it's wise to invest in PE-related stocks in the current environment, where the fading issue of high costs of capital that was permitting a return to market of PEs and was the main positive thing that 3i had in its favor as of our previous coverage is now back in force due to the reinflation effects of the Iran War. The declines in 3i's stock price have been justified particularly on tactical considerations around PE valuations and upward cost of capital pressure, and looking forward, we think the Action multiples are twice what they should be, and therefore, according to our assumptions, there would be no NAV discount and related margin of safety.

Valuation

Here are the top 10 investments by value for 3i, which uses a permanent prop capital model - they're not a typical GP whose business model depends on AUM, management fees, and carried interest, as they've raised money at the company level and use that to generate a return on investment. The total investment portfolio, including investments beyond the top 10, amounts to 30 billion GBP, and therefore a NAV of around 3,000 GBX, which is the way to assess the value of 3i at today's prices. The current share price is around 2,650 GBX. However, note the assumptions for the valuation of Action, the discount retailer and crown jewel of the 3i portfolio.

The multiple of 18.5x, net of the liquidity discount, remained unchanged, resulting in a valuation of £22,382 million for 3i’s 62.3% equity stake (30 September 2025: £21,464 million for a 60.1% stake).

Q3 performance update

These assumptions offer investors no margin of safety in our view. Consider other retailers, not even necessarily discount retailers carrying the sort of inventory Action carries, but things like clothes and groceries. Inditex (ISIN: ES0148396007) and owners of Zara are around 13x EV/EBITDA on a forward basis. They've had EBITDA growth of around 5% and are a very solid brand. Marks & Spencer (MAKSY) is at around 7x EV/EBITDA on a forward basis. They had 12% EBITDA growth. Europris ASA (ERPSY) is at 7x EV/EBITDA on a forward basis, and they have quite a similar type of inventory to Action. Note EBITDA growth of 7.5% for Europris. Action's EBITDA was up 14%, comparable to some of these picks (though a bit better than them), but with more than double the multiple at 18x, and that is after a liquidity discount. The stocks we are comparing Action to are already liquid. While it is true that Action is contemplating a US launch, it is competitive out there, and in principle these other stocks might also have markets that they still haven't tapped yet. Marks & Spencer is working on making their US strategy successful; they are struggling, but theoretically it's a new market for them as well. Their current growth is very comparable to Action. Ultimately, these are all retail stocks as well; the value-add in retail is not that great by our standards, where we prefer looking at things further up the value chain in general.

Action is more than 2/3 of the total 3i portfolio, so we do not judge 3i based on the provided NAV figure since we would not use the multiple they used for Action. We think a fairer NAV for 3i, assuming we'd have no problem with the valuations of the rest of the 3i portfolio, would be something below 2,000 GBX, which means 3i is trading at a premium, where there are plenty of discounted holdings out there with assets that we prefer.

Bottom Line

In circumstances where PE markets were seeing very frequent transactions, and considering the dry powder in the asset class, we may even be alright with a premium multiple applied in private markets, as 3i has done here. It hasn't been unheard of in the past that private market multiples were at premiums to public comps even though liquidity considerations would dictate the opposite should be the case. In our last coverage, we pointed out that there was some resumption in PE activity and M&A in general thanks to the rate cuts that finally came in 2025 in the US with inflation waning well below the 3% upper band, and that European PE had started to recover as well.

However, CPI readings are now at 3.3% in the US. Consumer sentiment is bad as well in the US because the Iran War has driven up prices at the pump, meaning inflation is front of mind for consumers and therefore likely baked into expectations. 3i is Europe-focused, but also Euro Area inflation is creeping up fast, and it has meaningfully impacted the yield curve, with 5-year rates up 25 bps and shorter-term rates up 50 bps since the beginning of the year. In fact, the latest data on global deal volumes from Axios shows a considerable toll taken by the Iran War on deal volumes already. With the blockade in place after failed ceasefire talks, the root of the energy crisis remains, and deal volumes may remain depressed, likely also in sponsor activity. We do not think that the PE markets are liquid enough yet to justify optimistic multiples, the likes of which we'd seen in 2021, which the industry thinks history will remember as a poor vintage.

We think there are plenty of better NAV plays out there.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Bargain EBITDA Multiples As GLP-1 Drama Passes
data.description:
In a surprisingly short amount of time, the S&P 500 has quickly shaken off the risk-off mentality and market jitters that have dominated since the conflict in Iran began. That said, many pockets of the stock market remain in deeply negative territory, particularly small- and mid-cap growth stocks, which have re-rated substantially downward.

Hims & Hers (HIMS), the personalized medications company, has seen one of the biggest re-ratings in the stock market to date. Shares are down nearly 40% since January, and relative to peaks above $60 briefly notched last year, the stock has lost two-thirds of its value. The question now is, when and how does Hims exit the bear market?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Hims in January, when the stock was trading at $33 per share. Needless to say, my bullishness was ill-timed, and I wasn't able to predict the sharp re-rating in small- and mid-cap growth that has taken place over the past few months. But when I take a look at this stock with fresh eyes, I see many appealing characteristics that make its freshly reduced valuation multiples a very compelling buy. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

With GLP-1 volatility in the rearview mirror, Hims & Hers' valuation is overdue for upward re-rating

As a reminder, over the past year, much of the volatility in Hims & Hers stock has concentrated around the unsure path to GLP-1 sales. Novo Nordisk, one of the principal makers of GLP-1 weight loss medications, initially sued Hims for infringing on its patent and selling an unauthorized compounded version of the drug after Novo exited its supply shortage status. In March, Novo Nordisk dropped its lawsuit against Hims & Hers. And then, beginning on May 26, Hims & Hers jointly announced with Novo Nordisk that it would be officially selling Wegovy and Ozempic on its platform (beginning at $149/month), effectively turning Hims & Hers into an authorized distribution channel for Novo Nordisk.

Meanwhile, an additional value-add revenue opportunity for Hims & Hers is a membership plan that is available as an add-on to weight loss medication. Priced at $149/month (on top of the regular cost of weight loss medication), this program gives patients access to ongoing medical check-ins, personalized nutrition plans that are meant to be used in conjunction with medication, and 24/7 support.

To me, the biggest upside opportunity for Hims & Hers is leaving the weight loss controversy behind in the rearview mirror. The company doesn't disclose exact revenue from weight loss, but notes that the majority of its revenue is outside this lower-margin specialty. With a favorable resolution for Hims & Hers with Novo Nordisk dropping its suit and agreeing to sell on the Hims & Hers platform, investors can now focus on the company's other growth catalysts: international expansion, growth of the Hers brand (which has now hit >$1 billion in ARR), and growth of new specialties. The company launched testosterone supplements in September 2025, perimenopause and menopause treatments in October, and diagnostic testing services in November.

With the distraction of GLP-1 behind us, Hims & Hers has a significant opportunity to re-rate its stock upward. At current share prices near $21, the stock trades at a market cap of $4.84 billion. Netting off the $928.8 million of cash against $972.6 million of convertible debt and $47.0 million of earn-outs due to M&A targets leaves us an enterprise value of $4.93 billion.

For FY26, Hims & Hers has guided to 15%-24% y/y growth in revenue to $2.7-$2.9 billion (we note the company should have higher visibility into its demand now than in FY25, when its continued sale of weight loss medication was still in question).

Where investors tripped up and penalized Hims was on its expectations for $300-$375 million in adjusted EBITDA, which is a wide 11%-13% range that represents downside from 13.5% margins in FY25.

Still, we note that we're compensated for risk in the form of a modest valuation at 14.6x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. The company is incurring near-term margin headwinds to expand into new overseas markets, which still represent a small contribution to revenue today. We have confidence in Hims & Hers' ability to scale profitably in new markets and new specialties, plus drive down corporate opex as a percentage of revenue. For a company that is still expecting to grow its top line at >20% y/y, Hims still represents "growth at a reasonable price."

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Hims' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Hims & Hers' revenue grew 28% y/y to $617.8 million, slightly beating Wall Street's expectations of $617.3 million in the quarter. Meanwhile, we note that the company has continued to deepen its bench of active subscribers. It ended FY26 with 2.5 million active subscribers, adding 282k net-new subscribers on a year/y basis. Meanwhile, full-year revenue grew 59% y/y, with international revenue growing ~5x y/y to $134 million (still a small 6% contribution to the overall top line).

Again, we have to emphasize here that Hims is making a deep marketing push into brand-new territory, primarily in Canada and large European markets (UK, Ireland, Germany, France, and Spain). This is what is causing margin drag in the near term in FY26, but it's a growth opportunity that substantially increases the company's TAM. Management believes it has an addressable population of 200+ million potential subscribers in Europe, versus 280 million in the U.S. and Canada.

Meanwhile, we note that the company recently announced its $1.15 billion acquisition of Eucalyptus (on a deferred payment schedule, with $240 million of consideration paid upfront and the rest through earn-outs), which is a digital healthcare platform with a large presence in Australia and Japan, which are brand-new markets for Hims & Hers. The deal is expected to close in mid-2026, and isn't included in the company's guidance targets.

Investors are nervous that international markets may take years to scale to profitability, and that the company isn't expecting Eucalyptus to be meaningfully accretive to profits in the near term. Per CEO Andrew Dudum's remarks on the Q4 earnings call explaining the rationale behind the deal:

Eucalyptus deploys a rigorous capital allocation framework similar to our own. They currently have an annual revenue run rate north of $450 million and strong execution enabled them to drive triple-digit year-over-year growth in each quarter of 2025, while also maintaining line of sight to profitability. While we do not expect the business to drive meaningful adjusted EBITDA losses, we do not expect to drive meaningful margin expansion for several years in our international business.

Across the majority of international markets, we expect to take a growth-oriented approach and focus on reaching as many consumers as possible before focusing on the margin expansion efforts, even if that means running certain markets at or near breakeven on an adjusted EBITDA margin basis. We saw this approach work in the U.S. and will utilize a similar playbook to progressively expand markets towards margin expansion in the future. This is our largest acquisition to date at up to $1.15 billion of total consideration. The upfront payment at close is expected to be approximately $240 million, with the remaining payments for guaranteed consideration and earnouts to be made through 2029.

Still, what gives us comfort is clear evidence that in mature markets, Hims & Hers can drive down opex as its subscriber pool grows. As shown in the chart below, marketing expenses reduced 6 points as a percentage of revenue to 38% in the most recent quarter:

This helped to offset a 5-point reduction in gross margins to 72% (due to a heavier mix of lower-margin weight loss sales), which positioned adjusted EBITDA at $66.3 million, reflecting 28% y/y growth at a flat 11% margin.

We expect Hims & Hers to continue scaling adjusted EBITDA margins gradually as its international markets mature, and the potential for the company to slow headcount growth (and achieve M&A synergies) by leveraging AI and automation also reflects potential upside. New high-margin offerings, such as diagnostic lab testing (which gives customers data on 130+ biomarkers), will also help the company's profitability profile. The company expects lab testing to hit $100 million in ARR in "the near future", not counting the cross-sell opportunity into recommended supplements and medications that the testing may reveal.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are risks that we need to keep in mind as well. As a reminder, Hims & Hers' moat lies in its ownership of the customer relationship, and in its base of subscribers (two-thirds of whom are on personalized plans) - but the company owns no IP. It may see its gross margin profile (currently in the low 70%s) erode further if consumers buy directly from pharmaceutical brands or from new marketplaces like TrumpRx.

We note as well that Hims & Hers is a scale business, and it doesn't have the capital to buy out its competitors in every new market it enters (its balance sheet won't support multiple Eucalyptus-size acquisitions). It may fail to scale in its new markets, which may put pressure on adjusted EBITDA margins in the long haul.

That said, I still see more potential upsides than downsides in this stock, especially at a low/mid-teens adjusted EBITDA multiple that underappreciates the stock's high-20%s growth rate. Stay long here and use this year's dip as a buying opportunity.
